description: The provided image is heavily blurred and completely unreadable as a scientific figure, with no identifiable features of material characterization data, micrographs, performance curves or schematics observed visually. It belongs to the study of Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C (x = 2.59, 5.84, 9.92, 11.58 wt%, denoted as 3Al, 6Al, 10Al, 12Al respectively) low density steels, which quantitatively evaluates contributions of solid solution strengthening, grain boundary strengthening, κ-carbide precipitation strengthening and ferrite strengthening to yield strength. The paper reports that yield strength increases significantly with Al content from 321 MPa for 3Al steel to 952 MPa for 12Al steel, and nanoscale κ-carbide precipitation is the dominant strengthening mechanism for high Al content steels. No specific content of this figure can be confirmed via direct visual observation.

This figure illustrates microstructural evolution of Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C low-density steels after 1100 °C 1 h solution treatment and water quenching. Subfigures a to d are optical microscopy (OM) images for 3Al, 6Al, 10Al and 12Al steels respectively: 3Al, 6Al, 10Al steels (a-c) exhibit full austenitic equiaxed grains with average grain size ~80 μm (twin boundaries excluded), while 12Al steel (d) shows austenite plus 7 vol% small blocky ferrite, with austenite grain size ~40 μm (twin boundaries excluded). Subfigures e and f are transmission electron microscopy (TEM) morphologies with corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns for 10Al and 12Al steels: weak superlattice spots in e confirm minor nano κ-(Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ carbide precipitation, while strong superlattice spots and modulated contrast in f verify abundant coherent nano κ-carbides. These results prove κ-carbides precipitate during quenching when Al content exceeds 10 wt%, with higher Al increasing precipitation driving force, matching the rising yield strength trend (321 MPa to 952 MPa as Al increases), supporting that κ-carbide precipitation strengthening is the dominant strengthening mechanism for high-Al low-density steels.

This figure presents mechanical properties of Fe-26Mn-6Al-1C (6Al) low-density steel under different solution treatment temperatures. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves for samples solution treated at 1000℃, 1100℃ and 1200℃: as solution temperature increases, the ultimate tensile strength decreases from over 820 MPa at 1000℃ to ~670 MPa at 1200℃, while fracture elongation increases from ~55% at 1000℃ to ~60% at 1200℃. Subplot (b) plots yield strength as a function of d^-1/2, the inverse square root of grain size, with experimental points and linear fit demonstrating a typical Hall-Petch relationship, where yield strength increases linearly with decreasing grain size, corresponding to higher d^-1/2 value. The figure confirms that grain size adjustment via changing solution treatment temperature introduces significant grain boundary strengthening effect, which is a key strengthening mechanism for the studied low-density steel.

This stacked bar chart illustrates the contribution of different strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength of Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C low-density steels with varying Al contents (3Al, 6Al, 10Al, 12Al, corresponding to ~2.59, 5.84, 9.92, 11.58 wt% Al, all processed by 1100 °C solution treatment for 1 h followed by water quenching). The x-axis represents Al content, while the y-axis denotes yield strength in MPa. The stacked segments correspond to base strength, Al solid solution strengthening, ferrite strengthening, grain refining strengthening, and κ-carbide precipitation strengthening, respectively. Total yield strength increases significantly with rising Al content, from ~321 MPa for 3Al steel to ~952 MPa for 12Al steel. 3Al and 6Al steels only gain contributions from base and Al solid solution strengthening, 10Al steel adds minor carbide precipitation strengthening, while 12Al steel obtains extra contributions from grain refining and ferrite strengthening, with carbide precipitation being the largest contributor. This figure confirms that increased Al content improves yield strength via synergistic multiple strengthening effects, especially κ-carbide precipitation strengthening.
